speaker: interviewee
text: --whatever. You do your thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and then introduce both of us. And then I'm off camera. You're on camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't see me anymore, other than in the beginning on the-- on the video.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the problem with the Yale-- the Yale project, where they do tend to-- or to-- how shall I put it?-- too much of the face.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the video? All the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I think, in general.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did the Yale one? Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're checking. We're checking the lav, checking the survivor's lav-- checking 1, 2, 3, 4, and five. Checking the interviewer's lav, interviewer. Channel 2, checking the mic, 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, what was the [? account ?] this is going to? This-- was it-- the [? account ?] has to be in Washington to go in the repository.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, I know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- some people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Slate it, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is March 13, 1995. The survivor is Ruth Keller. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Great Neck. The country-- the state is New York. And the country is United States of America. And the language is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape number 1. Say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 1. Hello, my name is Louise Bobrow-- L-O-U-I-S-E Bobrow, B-O-B-R-O-W. Today is March 13, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Ruth Keller, a survivor, in Great Neck, New York. And the country is United States of America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now take a deep breath.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like you to start out by giving me your complete name. And would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the name its Ruth, R-U-T-H, Keller, K-E double L- E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you give me your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruth Advokat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was spelled two, two ways. The Polish way is A-D-W-O-K-A-T. But we spelled it the German way, which is A-D-V-O-K-A-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Ruth, when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And is that the city that you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is the city that I lived in until we left in December, 1938. So I was 10 years old when I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like you to describe your life in Vienna. I know you were young. But could we begin by describing what you can remember of Vienna in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It tumbles. My main memory is, of course, the apartment with my parents and the extended family-- the aunts, the uncles, the cousins. My mother's family was extremely close. So my mother saw one of her sisters practically every single day. We all lived within walking distance. It may not be walking distance in the United States. But we walked longer distances in those days so that I was together with aunts and cousins as far back as I can remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We used to go away every summer, very early in the summer, and come back in the fall-- to small, country places very often. I remember some farms where my parents-- my mother would rent rooms in, in-- well, they were farms. But they had-- for guests, they were for city people as guests. And I used to look forward to these summers all year long, as far as I remember. Because it meant freedom, animals, and beautiful countryside, really. And I remember every fall coming back on the train and a, a feeling of grayness. To me, if you say Vienna, I would say, gray. Yes, I know many people-- many people think of the gorgeous city. And when I was a child, my childhood memories were the color of gray. Although my father used to take me for lovely walks on Sunday morning to all these sites and what's called the Innere Stadt with the beautiful parks. And I remember going to-- he took me to the museum. And I remember that the, the museum of art-- that beautiful museum of art-- I was always afraid to enter. And I could not. And so I put it out of mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I did not realize why until 1992, when I went back to Vienna and went in there-- why. The Baroque, the marbles, the overwhelming-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] how shall I put it? Extraneous [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] decoration that, that the building itself is, so that one loses sight. And to a little child, I figured that's why I was scared, the vastness of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that's just a memory. And even that didn't color it any better. I think perhaps because times were so bad. It was the early '30s. My memory goes back to the early '30s. It was Depression time. My father was struggling terribly to make a living. Money was very scarce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother's family tended to bemoan the good old days all the time. They were talking about the days before the war when they were well-to-do. And then the terrible things that happened during the war-- my grandfather died young in 1914 and left five children, who then had to fend for themselves. And my grandmother became ill. I think hindsight shows that she most likely had Alzheimer's because there was a change in personality besides other things. So that there were-- it was a very depressing atmosphere. And over the years, I-- once I saw a cartoon about this little boy who, wherever he went, there was a little cloud of rain. And it's raining on him. I remember he even went into a drawer. Came out of the drawer, it was raining on him. And that is how I remember Vienna. It was always raining on us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, in school, I went to public school in my neighborhood. By the way, we lived in-- not in the Jewish neighborhood of, of Vienna, which was the 2nd District. We lived in the 20th District. And that was a sort of a socialist, working-class district. It was the district where the Socialist Party put up the first Utopian housing project. And we were very few Jewish children in this public school. And when I went to public school in Austria, there was no separation of church and state, which means that, once a week, there was religious instruction. As the majority religion was Catholic, the non-Catholic children had to get up and leave the room. And I remember the jeers, the catcalls, the anti-Semitic remarks. And we were very few. I think we were no more than two or three to leave that classroom to go to religious instruction. And then when we got to that room, whenever it was, no matter what the male teacher tried to teach us, I remember nothing. I tuned it out because of my-- of the hurt incurred on the way out. And that was a weekly process. Also, the classroom teacher was an anti-Semite. And I remember that one year-- the 1st of May, you know, is supposed-- was supposed-- the tradition, you brought your teacher a bunch of lilies of the valley. And my mother gave me a bunch of lilies of the valley to give my teacher. And we all kind of walked up, you know. They were sitting. Their desks were on platforms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Some pedagogy they had there. Anyway, they was sitting up high. And we, the little children, walked by and gave her those little bouquets, which she then put at her desk. I remember it. And I came up. And she took that little bouquet and threw it right in the garbage. And then the next kid came up. So that's pretty much it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that for you, when that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't tell. But the fact that I remember it so vividly, and my memory not being that terrific shows you how that marked me. She was physically deformed. She was a hunchback. And I discussed this when I first-- when we were first married with my husband, I discussed it. And he explained to me that the reason why the New York City Board of Education, for instance-- in those days, anyway-- would not accept a physically-challenged person to become a teacher in elementary school was the fear that children might have and also the psychology of a person who might [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not really be open. I don't know whether that is a valid thing to do or not a valid thing. I can't judge. I really cannot judge. But I just know that this particular woman [SIGHS] was everything a teacher should not be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that it was definitely an anti-Semitic act--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it must have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by throwing the--

speaker: interviewee
text: There is no-- I was a good student.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had very good grades. I was a good student because that my-- as my father reminded me years later, and I'll come to that-- it wasn't, perhaps, that I was so smart. But I was a good student considering the, the student population in that particular district. So that was no great achievement. But, however, it couldn't be that I was a problem child, because I was not. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And with the other Jewish children in the class, did the same experience happen? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't remember. But there were-- maybe, maybe one other one-- another one. Maybe the other children who left with me were Protestant. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, do you remember the address-- the exact address of your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Othmargasse 42, Apartment 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was a small apartment. We could not afford anything fancy. It was very small, very modest. There were three Jewish-- I think there were only three Jewish families in that apartment house because I played with one girl on the-- on the first floor. She was a bit older than I was. And then, in the same apartment directly under us, was another Jewish family who eventually went to Palestine-- Israel. And then I met them again here. And we did speak about it then. And that family did not remember any other Jewish family either. So it was just these three families to the best of  our-- and, of course, we knew each other. I don't know who the other people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like--

speaker: interviewee
text: It is now a slum. And when I went in '92, there were Eastern people. I don't know if they are Turkish or Pakistani. But I went back to that particular apartment house, the front of which was all redone because, evidently, it had been bombed or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: The inside, the stairs-- I don't think they were-- [LAUGHS] they were cleaned since the day we left in 1938. And the, the door-- I said to my, my friend from Switzerland, I said, our door was a dark green. And would you believe the door was a dark green?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had intended to knock at the door and to introduce myself to whoever lived there. But when the super, the lady, the woman who came out-- and saw the two of us, who obviously did not look Near Eastern, didn't wear the kind of headdress that-- she had such fright in her eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The woman was practically shaking. May I help you, in a very poor German-- hardly could speak German. And what do you want?

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I looked at that scared face, it occurred to me that we may look like a couple of Nazis here, coming into-- maybe they were undocumented. Maybe these people weren't supposed to live there. So I kind of said, no, we're just walking up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I used to live here. And I don't-- she may not have understood me or not believed me. But she was following us in, in a very, very worried manner. So I just looked around and walked out again and figured-- and said to my friend, see? They threw us out. Look, look how far they have gotten since. Ruth, I'd like you to talk about your family now. First of all, tell me your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His-- we called him Joseph. His name was-- his given name was [NON-ENGLISH] Joseph Advokat. But we called him-- everybody called him Joseph, Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in business with his brother-in-law. They had a shoe store--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --on Berggasse, which is a street where Freud had-- a couple doors down, Freud had his-- lived, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you tell me about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worshipped my father. I absolutely worshipped my father. He was a great raconteur. He would tell stories. He was funny. He had a sense of humor. He did not-- he was like an antidote to the more gloomy side on my mother's family. He had a sunny outlook. He liked to play soccer on Sunday mornings. My mother thought that that was a very undignified thing to do. So he, he did that. He had a pickup soccer game very often. And he-- and I would watch and be so proud. And we'd keep it a secret from my mother. I'm sure it was no secret, but that kind of a game. And he took me for long walks. And my mother was very strictly kosher. And I thought everybody was kosher. But my father would, once in awhile, take me and buy me some of these goodies, which definitely were not. And this was sort of another secret between us. But I used to look forward-- he used to come home lunch because the store was-- I can't remember if the store was closed, or if he and his brother-in-law took different lunch hours because the big meal of the day was lunch, of course. And he would come home. And I would wait for him. And there was always a story. And there was always a story to tell. New story, Papa-- tell me a story. And there was always a story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me about your mother--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother--

speaker: interviewer
text: --her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Goldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her first name-- her, her Jewish name was Feige-- F-E-I-G-E. But she was called Fanny, Fanny. As a matter of fact, my cousins used to call her Tante [NON-ENGLISH] because [NON-ENGLISH] was a Viennese name. So, you know, her name was Fanny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] I understand that, when she was a teenager and after my, my-- after all, she was born in '97. And her father died in '14. So she was 17 years old. And they sent her to a commercial school. And she became a bookkeeper. And as a matter of fact, she kept the books for the store for a number of years. She was a bookkeeper. But she was talented. My mother sang well. My mother could look in a-- in a magazine-- she could look at a dress and buy, very cheaply, a little remnant. And then she would take, I remember, newspaper. And she would cut a pattern and make us all dresses. Sure, she made all my dresses. She made some of her sister's dresses. She made-- the only times I played with dolls, as a matter of fact, is when my mother made dresses. Otherwise, I had very little interest in dolls except when they didn't belong to me but to my cousin. Then I wanted those dolls. But the-- and she taught me how to knit and sew when I was very little. And I loved that. I enjoyed that very much. And she was a sensitive-- a very sensitive woman, very committed to Zionism, very, very committed to Palestine and to the thought of leaving all this behind. And someday-- her dream was someday, Eretz Yisrael.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sent me-- she sent me to a Hebrew school, which was twice a week after school and on Sunday mornings. And I understand. So they tell me that I became quite proficient in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been true because when we reached Belgium, my mother and my aunt-- the day after we arrived-- took me to school and to the Hebrew school. And the person-- I don't know if he was a rabbi or lay teacher who kind of tried to find out my level of Hebrew, then put me into a class with 16-year-old girls. I was 10. And that was really hell on wheels. And I cried. I really didn't want to go. And my parents couldn't quite understand. They thought it should be sort of a feather in my cap, didn't understand the real-- it was a dull-- you see, I, I was learning Hebrew. I used to know the answers. The kids were speaking Flemish. I was not good at Flemish. It-- I had a little gold chain, a tiny, tiny gold chain. And one big girl, big bully, ran after me out of rage or something. And she pulled the chain from the back. And I came home with that thin line of bleeding. That's when my mother liberated me from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back to your family for a minute. Do you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never had any brothers or sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you-- let me ask you this. What is your oldest ancestor that you remember? Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: On which side?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Liebe, Liebe Goldman. By that time, she was sick because she would sit in a-- an armchair. And she had a footstool. I remember the footstool. And she wore, wore slippers that were embroidered with pearls. And I would play with these pearls. And I understand I was an unruly child. And my mother had to discipline me a lot. And when I would come close to my grandmother, I felt very protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your other grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father's parents lived in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We never went to visit them. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, my grandfather [NON-ENGLISH], she's the only one I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --vaguely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your Jewish life in your home? Do you remember celebrating the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in a big way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos, definitely. I remember--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my father's Kiddush cup. I remember trying to lick the, the, the brandy that-- with which you make Kiddush. It was my, my mother was shomer Shabbos. My father and his brother-in-law had to keep the store open because things were so bad. And I know they felt badly about that. But I think Jewish lore or tradition says that, in order to make a living that one can trespass. I don't know if that is so or not. But my mother was strictly kosher. And it wasn't only that. The holidays were meaningful. As I said, I went to Hebrew school and to religious school. And I was told that, in my mother's family, there were some distinguished scholars. My mother's older brother, who was in this country-- that's the uncle who came here in 1933-- was a very pious Jew. But I loved him because it wasn't the, the letter of the law, which of course he followed, but it was the spirit-- his kindness, his ability to forgive, and yes, it was a very strictly Jewish home. My father came from a less-observant environment. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he, he followed whatever. Of course, he had a-- he had a good Jewish background. It goes without saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Pesach? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was able to stay up for the second part. [LAUGHS] I remember that. My father had, also, a beautiful voice. And he sang. And he sang it. And I loved it. We had-- of course, we had a double set of dishes for Passover only-- the milchig and the fleishig. And I helped my mother bring down these-- these were the beautiful dishes, not what we used all year but the Pesach dishes. I remember one set with little, tiny red roses. And it was a thrill to take it out of the cupboard and wash them and, and use them. And I got a new dress. I would get a new dress. And, and I remember always-- I think it's Shavuoth that we had the peonies. And then my mother got peonies for the tiny apartment that we had. And it was very festive. And Rosh Hashanah, they went to-- on Rosh Hashanah, my father wore a kittel, which is that white shroud that they wear. And men were separated from women. And we children used to play outside. And it was always a small shul. And that was a sad occasion because my mother mourned her parents very deeply-- very deeply. And, of course, it came out on Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say about Jewish life in Vienna in general? Was there a very active Jewish community in Vienna that you can remember as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I imagine there must be because where my-- both my aunts lived in the 2nd District, it was very Jewish. Everybody sort of appeared to be Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But as for remembering beyond that, not much. I remember the neighborhood temple. I remember the special shul for the High Holy Days. I remember the services. And everybody was Jewish, other than the children I went to school with. So at Christmastime, I remember being invited and being very impressed with a Christmas tree, and-- and so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I also remember the talk of the anti-Semitism at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I'm going to ask you about that in a minute. Before we go on to that, I want to just ask you, what was the language that was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- I think-- if I remember correctly, my mother spoke Yiddish-- Yiddish-German, German-Yiddish-- with her sisters. My father spoke German, you know, the Viennese. He could speak Yiddish, too, of course. But at home-- my house, German was spoken, the Viennese-type German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did, did you say-- you did not have any brothers or sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: --correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What would you say was the relationship that existed, prior to the Nazis coming in, of the Jew and the non-Jew in Vienna? What-- how were-- how were relationships? How would you characterize them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Perhaps in adult circles, there was some. I can remember none in my family, other than the people in the apartment house or people that one-- at the market, and so on and so forth. But as for friends or acquaintances, there were no non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in general, would you say people got along well-- Jew and non-Jew? How would you characterize their--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they must have. They must have gotten along well because there were not the pogroms that one heard of in Poland or Russia. There was this kind of a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] living. And from what I read-- from the history, many Jews-- I mean, in the 19th century, Vienna was a very enlightened city. Jews prospered. Jews became very prominent. And so that-- there must have been an atmosphere of tolerance. As a matter of fact, there was. From all that I read, there was. On the other hand, there was a great-- whenever there is this ambience of tolerance, there's a great deal of assimilation that takes place so that many, many Jews-- Viennese Jews, and perhaps others, too-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] became wannabe Christians. Nobody changed their names. That was not America. People I don't think changed their names. But I-- as for the, the interaction between non-Jews and Jews, I only know that from reading and history and not of personal--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not personal? I was going to ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you have some non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. From, from school, there were-- one of two girls, I can't remember their name, who would come to my apartment and who my mother allowed me to go to their apartment and play. And that, to me, was, was fine because I didn't have that many friends in, in the apartment house. As I said, the girl was older. And the boy was of no interest to me. We [LAUGHS] didn't play the same games. So-- but then I-- so that was during the school year, really, because then we were-- my mother always went to the country with some relative or other. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had non-Jewish friends. And you were-- good relationships existed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, from the-- to the best of my recollection. Other than-- but there's the other side. Because on the way to school from, from this Othmargasse to-- I had to cross a, a main street. And then there was a shortcut to school through the yard of a church. It was a big, beautiful church. And it was a pretty park all around it. I could not do that. I could never cross that park.
